headline: The secret life of the sumo topknot
summary: Nothing gets between a wrestler and his hairstylist
body_text:
There are many rules that govern sumo wrestling, the Japanese sport that operates on the principle of pushing another man down or out of a circular clay ring. But what epitomises wrestlers, in Japan at least, is their distinctive topknot.

The mage topknots originated around the 17th century, favoured by the Samurai warrior class. When the Edo period ended in the mid-19th century, however, the warrior class was abolished along with the last shogunate’s government. In the Meiji era the new government, eager to modernise its society, passed an 1871 law that allowed people to choose their own hairstyle. It accelerated the trend among men to do away with their mage and wear their hair short. Yet despite the national phenomenon, the sumo wrestlers still preserved the mage.

The number of professional sumo wrestlers in Japan peaked at 943 in 1994; today, the number is closer to 600, with 2024 seeing the lowest number of hopefuls taking part in the Japan Sumo Association’s test for recruits.
